coredata.dc:title: Wireless electrochemical and luminescent detection of bacteria based on surface-coated CsWO3-immobilized fluorescent carbon dots with photothermal ablation of bacteria
coredata.dc:creator: Robby, Akhmad Irhas
Kim, Seul Gi
Lee, Un Han
In, Insik
Lee, Gibaek
Park, Sung Young
coredata.dc:description: Simple and rapid simultaneous detection and killing of bacteria is crucial for addressing health issues related to pathogenic bacteria. Here, we describe the design of a wireless electrochemical and luminescent sensor for bacteria detection using surface-coatable electrochemically generated fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs), which were synthesized from a cationic polymer. The FCDs were further integrated with near-infrared (NIR)-responsive cesium tungsten oxide (CsWO3) by utilizing catechol moieties on the FCD surface for photothermal-based antibacterial activity. The CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids showed strong fluorescence emission, which was quenched due to interaction between the cationic FCD surface and the anionic bacterial cell wall. This interaction could also be observed electrochemically according to the change in resistance values before and after binding with bacteria. In this study, the limit of detection (LOD) was determined at 70 CFU/mL for Escherichia coli and 131 CFU/mL for Staphylococcus aureus using the luminescent method and <10 CFU/mL for both bacterial strains using the electrochemical method, indicating the electrochemical approach as the more sensitive method. Additionally, the electrochemical-based bacterial detection assay can be developed into a wireless bacteria-sensing system, which can be easily monitored in real-time via a smartphone. Furthermore, this nanohybrid was able to cause photothermolysis of bacteria upon NIR exposure owing to the photothermal conversion of CsWO3, which killed almost 100% of E. coli and S. aureus using only 1 mg/mL of CsWO3–FCD. Thus, this nanohybrid offers a novel approach for dual electrochemical- and luminescent-based detection of bacteria along with high antibacterial activity.

(c) SEM-EDX images and elemental percentage for FCD and CsWO3-FCD nanohybrids. (d) TEM images and (e) XRD patterns of CsWO3-FCD nanohybrids.

PL spectra of CsWO3-FCD nanohybrids (1 mg/mL) in the presence of (a) E. coli and (b) S. aureus at various concentrations (101-107 CFU/mL). Excitation wavelength (λex) was 400 nm with maximum emission wavelength (λem) at 440 nm. (c) Confocal images of CsWO3-FCD nanohybrids (1 mg/mL) before and after treatment with bacteria (107 CFU/mL). (d) Flow cytometric analysis of CsWO3-FCD nanohybrids alone (1 mg/mL) and in the presence of bacteria (107 CFU/mL).

(a) Contact angles of FCD and CsWO3-FCD nanohybrids coated on PP, PET, PVC, and Si surfaces. (b) Confocal images of CsWO3-FCD nanohybrids coated on a PP surface, in the presence and absence of different concentrations of bacterial solution (Scale bar is 50 μm).
SEM images of CsWO3-FCD nanohybrids coated on a Si surface, in the presence and absence of NIR irradiation, for the detection and killing of bacteria.

(a) Resistance of CsWO3-FCD nanohybrids coated on an Si surface, in the presence and absence of bacteria, based on LCR meter measurements. The change in resistance in the presence of (b) E. coli and (c) S. aureus was dependent on bacterial concentration (101–107 CFU/mL).
EIS spectra of CsWO3-FCD nanohybrids coated on an Si surface, in the presence of (a) E. coli and (b) S. aureus at different bacterial concentrations (101–107 CFU/mL).

(a) Schematic illustration and (b) photograph of the wireless sensor for electrochemical-based bacterial detection. Schematic illustration of (a) the fabrication of CsWO3-FCD nanohybrids and (b) the application of CsWO3-FCD nanohybrids for a wireless electrochemical- and luminescent-based bacterial sensing system, with photothermal ablation of bacteria.

head.ce:title: Wireless electrochemical and luminescent detection of bacteria based on surface-coated CsWO3-immobilized fluorescent carbon dots with photothermal ablation of bacteria

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Simple and rapid simultaneous detection and killing of bacteria is crucial for addressing health issues related to pathogenic bacteria. Here, we describe the design of a wireless electrochemical and luminescent sensor for bacteria detection using surface-coatable electrochemically generated fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs), which were synthesized from a cationic polymer. The FCDs were further integrated with near-infrared (NIR)-responsive cesium tungsten oxide (CsWO3) by utilizing catechol moieties on the FCD surface for photothermal-based antibacterial activity. The CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids showed strong fluorescence emission, which was quenched due to interaction between the cationic FCD surface and the anionic bacterial cell wall. This interaction could also be observed electrochemically according to the change in resistance values before and after binding with bacteria. In this study, the limit of detection (LOD) was determined at 70 CFU/mL for Escherichia coli and 131 CFU/mL for Staphylococcus aureus using the luminescent method and <10 CFU/mL for both bacterial strains using the electrochemical method, indicating the electrochemical approach as the more sensitive method. Additionally, the electrochemical-based bacterial detection assay can be developed into a wireless bacteria-sensing system, which can be easily monitored in real-time via a smartphone. Furthermore, this nanohybrid was able to cause photothermolysis of bacteria upon NIR exposure owing to the photothermal conversion of CsWO3, which killed almost 100% of E. coli and S. aureus using only 1 mg/mL of CsWO3–FCD. Thus, this nanohybrid offers a novel approach for dual electrochemical- and luminescent-based detection of bacteria along with high antibacterial activity.

ce:section.ce:para: Detection and visualization of bacteria are crucial for diagnostics, healthcare applications, and basic scientific research [1,2]. Due to the rapid growth of nanobiotechnology, several methods have been developed for imaging and detecting bacteria by utilizing various staining techniques, including fluorescent dyes [3,4] and semiconductor quantum dots [5,6]. These strategies are widely used and commercially available but suffer from several drawbacks, including their being time consuming, having a high limit of detection (LOD), and requiring expensive equipment. Therefore, there is a pressing need for the development of technically simple, inexpensive, and sensitive strategies that can be employed for a broad range of bacterial species.

Luminescent nanoparticles have had an exceptional impact on the development of diverse area, such as photonics, energy, electronics, and medicine [7–10]. Among the different photoluminescent materials, fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) have recently emerged and garnered a lot of interest because of their low toxicity, low environmental impact, and simple synthetic routes [11,12]. FCDs possess high chemical and photostability, high conductivity, superior water dispersibility, and attractive fluorescence characteristics. [13–15]. These unique properties make FCDs promising nanomaterials in the fields of nanobiotechnology, biological labeling, photocatalysis, sensing, and biomedicine; however, FCDs showing high selectivity toward bacteria have not been designed. Therefore, further investigation is required to functionalize and modify FCDs as selective bacterial sensors.

FCDs can be readily combined with silica nanoparticles, metal oxides, or polymers to produce fluorescent nanohybrids with diverse functionalities [16–18]. Recently, FCDs were employed to detect/label bacteria in solution [19–22] but have rarely been employed on a coated surface. For practical application, it is highly desirable to detect bacteria in the solid state. Thus, modification of the FCD surface through conjugation with surface-coatable materials is necessary. For that purpose, several surface-coatable polymers/materials such as polydopamine or catechol can be successfully utilized to synthesize surface-coatable FCDs in order to obtain the expected functionality [23–25]. Moreover, combining FCDs with antibacterial agents such as metal nanoparticles or near infrared (NIR)-responsive nanoparticles, is a promising strategy for designing simple and versatile bacterial sensors with simultaneous antibacterial activity [26–29].

The purpose of the present work was to design an effective, sensitive, and versatile method for the simultaneous detection and killing of bacteria. For this purpose, we used an electrochemical- and luminescent-based approach that utilizes catechol-functionalized, cationic, electrochemically generated FCDs combined with cesium tungsten oxide (CsWO3) nanoparticles to support photothermal-based antibacterial activity. CsWO3 is a tungsten bronze-type compound (MxWO3, where M represents elements such as Cs, K, and Na) that shows localized surface plasmon resonance, leading to strong NIR absorption, thereby making it an excellent option for photothermal-based bacterial killing [28–30]. Luminescence assay relies on a rational change in fluorescence intensity, whereas electrochemical-based bacterial detection occurs through changes in resistance following binding of the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrid to bacteria. Additionally, to obtain real-time bacterial detection, we developed a wireless electrochemical sensing system that enables direct observation of results via a smartphone. Our results showed that this nanohybrid material effectively killed bacteria due to photothermal heat generation from the NIR-responsive CsWO3 nanoparticle. This method represents a novel approach for electrochemical- and luminescent-based bacterial detection combined with a wireless sensing system that also demonstrates a high level of antibacterial activity, allowing simultaneous bacterial detection and eradication.

Polyethylene glycol (PEG; Mw = 3,500), 2-(dimethylamino)ethyl methacrylate (DMA), bromoethane, 2-chloro-3,4-dihydroxyacetophenone (CCDP), t-butyl peroxybenzoate, sulfuric acid, ethanol, diethyl ether, toluene, [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide] (MTT), Tris-buffered saline (TBS), MRS medium, and Luria–Bertani (LB) medium were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, South Korea. A penicillin–streptomycin solution, RPMI 1640 medium, 0.25% (w/v) trypsin, fetal bovine serum, and 0.05% (w/v) EDTA were purchased from Gibco, South Korea. Propidium iodide (PI) and SYTO9 were purchased from Molecular Probes, Life Technologies (Invitrogen, Carlsbad, CA, USA). Silicon (Si) wafer (P-type) was purchased from Silicon Technology Corporation (Daejeon, South Korea).

1H-nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were obtained using an Avance 400 MHz spectrometer (Bruker, Billerica, MA, USA). UV–vis spectra were recorded using an Optizen 2020UV spectrophotometer (Mecasys Co., Daejeon, Korea). Particle size was measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS; Zetasizer Nano; Malvern Panalytical, Herrenberg, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained using an Omicrometer ESCALAB instrument (Omicrometer, Taunusstein, Germany). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using an L550B luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, Waltham, MA, USA). An infrared camera (Thermo Tracer TH9100; NEC Avio Infrared Technologies, Tokyo, Japan) with an 808-nm NIR laser (PSU-III-LRD; CNI Optoelectronics Tech. Co. Ltd., Beijing, China) was used to examine photothermal effect. A FilterMax F3 multimode microplate reader (Molecular Devices, San Jose, CA, USA) was used for MTT assay. Analyses of live and dead bacteria were conducted using a Zeiss LSM 510 confocal laser scanning microscope (Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany). Water-contact angles were measured using a DO3210 instrument (KRUSS Ltd., Hamburg, Germany). Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images were observed using a JSM-6700F SEM (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was measured using an AXS ADVANCE D-8, Bruker. Electrochemical analysis was performed using an LCR meter (4100; Wayne Kerr Electronics, Bognor Regis, UK) and an electrochemical impedance spectrometer (EIS; CS350; CorrTest Instruments, Wuhan, China). For wireless system, a wireless device, comprising a micro-controller (Arduino Uno; ATmega328P Processor) as the sensor and a smartphone as the real-time data receiver and display, was used. A Bluetooth module (AppGosu; http://www.appgosu.com/) was used as the wireless connection between the smartphone and the micro-controller [31].

NIR-responsive CsWO3 nanoparticles were synthesized according to a previously described method using cesium carbonate (Cs2CO3) and ammonium tungstate [(NH4)10H2(W2O7)6] [28,30]. Briefly, (NH4)10H2(W2O7)6 and Cs2CO3 were dissolved and stirred in DDW at room temperature for 1 h. This mixture was transferred to ceramic crucible for heating in a heating chamber for 8 h at 180 °C. The obtained powder was then heated at 500 °C for 1 h, followed by further annealing using vacuum furnace (800 °C, 1 h, N2 atmosphere).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of bromoethane- and CCDP-quaternized PEG-grafted PDMA (B/C-PgP)
ce:section.ce:para: PgP was synthesized according to a previously described method [32]. For the quaternization process, PgP (16.07 g), CCDP (1.43 g), and bromoethane (9.8 g) were dissolved in 90 mL of anhydrous ethanol and stirred at 70 °C for 12 h under N2 atmosphere. After completion of reaction, the solvent was evaporated using rotary evaporator and further precipitated in cold diethyl ether. Subsequently, the product was dried in a vacuum oven to obtain a yield of 94.8%. This synthesis protocol is presented as Scheme S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of FCDs from B/C-PgP carbonization
ce:section.ce:para: FCD was synthesized according to a reported procedure [33]. B/C-PgP (1 g) was mixed with DDW (5 mL) and H2SO4 36 N (10 mL) and then stirred for 1 min at room temperature. The solution was then poured into 185 mL of DDW and dialyzed for 24 h against water (Mw cut-off = 3,500). The solution was then freeze-dried.
CsWO3 nanoparticles (0.09 g) were dissolved with FCDs (1.8 g) in 50 mL of TBS buffer at pH 8.5 and reacted for 24 h at room temperature. Finally, the sample was freeze-dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence assay for bacterial detection using the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrid in an aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: For solution-state luminescent-based detection, bacterial solutions containing Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus (101–107 CFU/mL) were cultured in LB and MRS media, respectively, at 37 °C for 24 h. Different amounts of CsWO3–FCDs (0.1–5 mg/mL) were then added into the bacterial suspensions (1 × 107 CFU/mL) and incubated for 1 h. Suspensions were centrifuged and washed with PBS pH 7.4 three times. To detect bacteria, the cell pellets were labeled with CsWO3–FCDs and resuspended in 1 mL PBS pH 7.4. Fluorescence was measured in PL spectrometer at an excitation wavelength of 440 nm, also using flow cytometer and confocal microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical- and luminescent-based bacterial detection on a coated surface
ce:section.ce:para: The luminescent-based assay was performed using CsWO3–FCD-coated polypropylene (PP) film containing each bacterial strain. The CsWO3–FCD-coated PP film was prepared according to a reported method [34]. After 1 h incubation, the coated PP film was taken out and washed with PBS for further drying and measuring its fluorescence property.

For the electrochemical method, bacterial detection was performed using an LCR meter and an EIS. The prepared CsWO3–FCD-coated Si wafer containing bacteria was connected to electrodes, and the resistance values were compared before and after binding with bacteria. To understand the correlation between the number of bacteria and the change in resistance, flow cytometric analysis (fluorescence-activated cell sorting [FACS]) was performed to count the number of attached bacteria on the coated surface (Scheme S2). The initial number of bacteria was determined using FACS (data a), and the bacterial solution was then incubated with the CsWO3–FCD-coated Si wafer at 37 °C for 1 h. Subsequently, the bacteria-treated surface was washed with PBS (pH 7.4; 1 mL, three times) to detach unbound bacteria. After washing, the solution was collected and analyzed by FACS to count the number of unbound bacteria (data c). The total number of bacteria attached to the surface (data b) was calculated as follows:
Attached bacteria (data b, CFU/mL) = Initial (data a) – unbound bacteria (data c)

LOD for luminescent- and electrochemical-based bacterial detection was determined by recording signals from gradually increased concentrations of bacteria (101–107 CFU/mL). The LOD value was calculated based on linear regression analysis and corresponded to the linear calibration curve between bacterial concentration and signals (S/N = 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacterial detection on a coated surface using a wireless sensor with a smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: We developed a wireless system for electrochemical-based bacterial detection by enabling the real-time result to be directly viewed on a smartphone. Briefly, the CsWO3–FCD-coated film labeled bacteria were connected to the wireless device. The result (resistance graph) was displayed on the smartphone through the Bluetooth connection.

The efficiency of bacterial killing by CsWO3–FCDs (0.1–5.0 mg/mL) was measured under NIR irradiation using an MTT assay. Bacteria killing efficiency in this context describes the number of bacteria that can be killed as a result of the photothermal effect following irradiation of the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrid with an NIR laser. S. aureus and E. coli cells (1 × 107 CFU/mL) were incubated with CsWO3–FCD (0.1–5 mg/mL) for 1 h and irradiated with an NIR laser (5 min, 2 W/cm2) at 37 °C. The bacterial suspension was then centrifuged and washed with PBS before additional 4-h incubation with MTT solution (20 μL, 5 mg/mL in PBS). A 180 μL MTT-solubilizing agent was added and then put in shaking incubator for 15 min. Absorbances were measured at 570 nm on a Varioskan Flash microplate reader (Thermo Electron Corporation, Waltham, MA, USA). Cell viability was calculated by comparing with control (cells without NIR irradiation). The live/dead bacteria were estimated by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) and flow cytometry using SYTO9 (live) and PI staining (dead).

Bacterial suspensions were centrifuged and washed with an ethanol gradient (25, 50, 75, and 99%) consecutively. Bacterial solution (20 μL) was then drop-casted onto an Si wafer, dried for 3 h, and then subjected for SEM analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concept of material design and its mechanism and applications
ce:section.ce:para: The outer membrane of bacteria has a negative charge; therefore, based on interactions between positively charged materials and the negatively charged bacterial wall, it is possible to design a novel platform for detecting bacteria. In this study, FCDs were rationally synthesized through controlled carbonization of B/C-PgP, which resulted in FCDs with a positively charged surface due to the quaternized bromoethane and catechol moieties (Scheme 1 a). Use of a catechol- and bromoethane-conjugated polymer (PgP polymer) as a precursor for constructing functionalized FCDs provided an sp2-rich carbon bond which is critical for fluorescence and electrochemical bacterial detection, as well as for immobilization of CsWO3, surface coatability, and imparting positive charge to the CsWO3-FCD nanohybrid [32,35]. Carbonization using a strong acid (concentrated H2SO4) allows functionalization of multi-luminescent and conductive nanohybrid materials via the partially carbonized polymer [31,36]. The FCDs were then combined with NIR-responsive CsWO3 nanoparticles via the interaction between metal nanoparticles and catechol moieties, making this nanohybrid material is potential for simultaneous sensing and photothermal killing of bacteria. The adhesive catechol moieties on the FCD surface showed excellent surface-coatable properties, which were then utilized for the detection of bacteria, even in a solid state. The dual-state detection of bacteria (solution and coated surface) is described in Scheme 1b. For the luminescent-based method, the synthesized CsWO3–FCD nanohybrid exhibited strong fluorescence intensity that was quenched after binding with bacteria. This binding phenomenon can also be detected using an electrochemical method that measures changes in resistance values. Furthermore, the electrochemical method can be developed for wireless bacterial sensing, with data acquired directly via a smartphone. Additionally, the CsWO3–FCD simultaneously showed antibacterial effects against gram-positive and -negative strains under NIR irradiation by generating photothermal heat. Hence, CsWO3–FCD has the potential to kill bacteria in the presence of NIR and possesses a unique feature for the bacteria detection on coated surface due to the presence of adhesive catechol moieties.

The chemical structure of the synthesized polymer and the FCD was determined from 1H NMR spectra (Fig. S1a). The data showed peaks at 0.8 [3H, –CH3], 1.25 [2H, –CH2–], 2.5 [3H, –CH3–N], 3.5 [2H, N–CH2], 3.7 [PEG], and 4.2 [2H, –CH2–N], clearly indicating the PgP polymer backbone. Additionally, aromatic peaks from 6.7 ppm to 7.5 ppm indicated successful quaternization of the catechol group. The presence of catechol peaks in the 1H NMR spectrum, even after carbonization, clearly demonstrated the existence of catechol moieties on the surface of the FCD. To gain insight into the optical properties of the synthesized nanohybrid, we performed UV–vis and PL spectroscopy analyses. FCDs showed two absorption peaks at ~255 nm and ~360 nm, corresponding to π–π* transition of the aromatic ring in the catechol structure (Fig. 1 a) [32,35,37,38]. These results clearly confirmed the presence of a catechol moiety on the surface of the FCD.

However, after fabrication of the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrid, the 360-nm peak blue-shifted due to the interaction between the catechol moiety and the metal nanoparticle. The presence of a peak in the visible-to-NIR region signified the existence of CsWO3 nanoparticles. The PL spectra for the FCDs and CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids (Fig. S1b, c) revealed that the FCDs exhibited excellent blue-emission features (λex max = 380 nm; λem max = 435 nm) and showed excitation-dependent emission behavior (Fig. S1b) [39]. Additionally, the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrid displayed blue emission (λex max = 400 nm; λem max = 440 nm) (Fig. S1c), which prompted the use of this material for detection and photothermal killing of bacteria. The PL QY% for the FCD and CsWO3–FCD, evaluated relative to quinine sulfate used as a standard [40], was 6.71% and 6.05%, respectively.

To analyze the size distribution of the CsWO3–FCD, we performed DLS analysis (Fig. 1b). The average size of the FCD and CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids was calculated at 21.04 nm and 164.20 nm, respectively. The increase in the size of the nanohybrid clearly signified the successful immobilization of CsWO3 nanoparticles on the FCD. Zeta potential analysis indicated that the surface charges of the FCD and CsWO3–FCD were ~+16.23 mV and ~+11.19 mV, respectively, suggesting the presence of the positively charged surface of the nanohybrid for targeting the negatively charged bacterial cell wall (Fig. S2). Moreover, the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids demonstrated good stability in water according to their stable UV–vis absorbance at 360 nm for up to 10 days (Fig. S3).

We then examined the surface morphology and elemental composition by SEM-EDX and TEM (Fig. 1c, d). SEM and TEM images showed a round-shaped morphology for the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids, with an average size correlating with the DLS results. Furthermore, elemental Cs and W were clearly observed in the nanohybrid after EDX elemental mapping, with a lattice structure of CsWO3–FCD indicated by d-spacing of 0.30 nm (FCD) and 0.37 nm (CsWO3). XRD analysis was then performed to obtain additional structural information (Fig. 1e). The appearance of characteristic CsWO3 peaks in the 2θ range of 24° to 37° demonstrated successful immobilization of the CsWO3 nanoparticles. To clearly understand the structural features of the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrid, we then performed XPS (Fig. S4). The full scan spectra (Fig. S4a) clearly indicated that the nanohybrid comprised five elements, including C1s, N1s, O1s, W4f, and Cs3d, with the characteristic binding peaks of both Cs and W more obvious in the deconvoluted spectra (Fig. S4b, c). The C1s spectrum (Fig. S4d) was deconvoluted into six spectra corresponding to CC (284.2 eV), C–C (284.9 eV), C–N (285.9 eV), C–O (286.2 eV), CO (287.0 eV), and O–CO (288.2 eV). The existence of sp2 and sp3 carbon demonstrated that the FCDs were retained after immobilization. The N1s spectrum (Fig. S4e) showed two peaks for N0 (401.4 eV) and N+ (402.2 eV), whereas the O1s spectrum (Fig. S4f) showed peaks for W–O (528.8 eV), CO (531.9 eV), and C–O (533.2 eV) [41–44]. Additionally, Raman spectra showed a D-band (1340 cm−1) and G-band (1543 cm−1) for CsWO3 − FCD, with an ID:IG ratio of 0.62 (Fig. S5). This indicated a higher number of sp2 bonds in the CsWO3–FCD due to the presence of FCD, which was understandably related to the electroconductivity of the nanohybrid [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescent-based bacterial detection and photothermal properties in solution
ce:section.ce:para: For the detection of bacteria in solution, the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrid was employed to bacterial solution containing E. coli (gram-negative bacteria) or S. aureus (gram-positive bacteria). Firstly, bacteria solutions (107 CFU/mL) were treated with CsWO3–FCDs (0.1–5 mg/mL), and the fluorescence intensity was measured (λex = 400 nm; λem = 440 nm) and compared with that of the control (CsWO3–FCDs, 5 mg/mL). The fluorescence intensity of the CsWO3–FCD-treated E. coli and S. aureus was significantly quenched at all CsWO3–FCD concentrations (Fig. S6a, b). Furthermore, various E. coli and S. aureus concentrations (101–107 CFU/mL) incubated with a fixed concentration of CsWO3–FCD solution (1 mg/mL) again resulted in markedly quenched fluorescence intensities relative to the control (CsWO3-FCD solution, 1 mg/mL) (Fig. 2 a, b). The LOD, obtained from a linear calibration curve (Fig. S7), for this luminescent-based bacterial detection system was 70 CFU/mL for E. coli and 131 CFU/mL for S. aureus. Moreover, we observed the emission changes in CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids after interacting with bacteria via CLSM and FACS analysis [46]. As can be seen in Fig. 2c, the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids showed a clear blue emission in the absence of bacteria, whereas the fluorescence intensity was almost completely quenched when treated with E. coli or S. aureus. FACS analysis (Fig. 2d) revealed that the mean fluorescence intensity of the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids decreased from 190 to 139 after treated with E. coli and from 190 to 153 after treated with S. aureus. Thus, the fluorescence-quenching phenomenon clearly demonstrated the interaction between the cationic surface of the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids and anionic sites on the bacterial cell wall.

We hypothesized that the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids exert antibacterial activity triggered by NIR irradiation owing to the availability of NIR-responsive CsWO3 nanoparticles and thereby generate a photothermal effect potentially causing photothermolysis of bacterial cells [35]. The photothermal effect of the CsWO3–FCDs (1 mg/mL) was assessed according to time (0–5 min) and laser power (0.5–2 W/cm2) under NIR irradiation. As shown in Fig. S8, increases in laser power and time resulted in a temperature elevation (ΔT) in the CsWO3–FCDs (1 mg/mL) up to 28 °C at 5 min of irradiation. We then evaluated the photothermal performance of the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids (0.1–5 mg/mL) before and after incubation with a bacterial solution under NIR irradiation (808 nm; 2 W/cm2). As shown in Fig. S9a, the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids showed greater ΔT than FCDs, along with a ΔT of ~40 °C for 5 mg/mL of CsWO3–FCDs after 5 min NIR irradiation. These results can be attributed to the light-absorptive behavior of plasmonic CsWO3 nanoparticles [28]. Additionally, in the presence of bacterial solution (107 CFU/mL) (Fig. S9b, c), the ΔT of 5 mg/mL CsWO3–FCD solution was ~27 °C. This less pronounced increase in temperature signified the use of released heat for the photothermal killing of bacteria.

We then examined the bacterial killing efficiency of the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids toward E. coli and S. aureus using an MTT assay at various CsWO3–FCD concentrations (0.1–1 mg/mL) and in the presence and absence of NIR irradiation. In the absence of NIR irradiation, bacterial cell viability of E. coli and S. aureus were high (90–100%), indicating that the CsWO3–FCD showed low cytotoxicity (Fig. S10a). By contrast, in the presence of NIR irradiation, the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids showed photothermal killing ability towards E. coli and S. aureus, as indicated by a decrease in cell viability along with increases in CsWO3–FCD concentration. This result verified that NIR exposure enhanced the antibacterial activity of the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids due to their photothermal effect. Furthermore, we observed that the bacterial killing efficiency was more effective when the laser power and irradiation time were increased (Fig. S10b, c), as higher ΔT caused more damage to the bacteria cell wall [47,48]. Moreover, we evaluated photothermal killing of bacteria by confocal live/dead imaging and flow cytometry analysis using SYTO9 (green) staining of live bacteria and PI (red) staining of dead bacteria. The confocal images of E. coli and S. aureus with and without NIR irradiation (Fig. S10d) revealed a large number of red-stained (dead) cells in the presence of CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids treated with bacteria under 5 min NIR exposure, whereas in the absence of NIR, a large number of green-stained cells (live) were observed. This result confirmed that the generation of heat due to NIR irradiation promoted bacterial killing. We then assessed bacterial viability after NIR irradiation using flow cytometry (Fig. S10e). The results showed a large percentage of viable bacterial cells in the absence of the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids and NIR exposure, whereas in the presence of the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids and 5 min of NIR exposure, the dot distribution shifted from the lower quadrant (live/SYTO9 staining) to the upper quadrant (dead/PI staining), confirming effective bacterial killing [49]. Furthermore, we found that the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids (0.001–5 mg/mL) demonstrated good biocompatibility in the absence of NIR irradiation, which was confirmed by high levels of cell viability of CsWO3–FCD-treated MDCK cells (Fig. S10f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescent-based detection of bacteria on the CsWO3–FCD-coated surface and its antibacterial activity
ce:section.ce:para: To establish a luminescent method for bacterial sensing on a coated surface, we determined the water-contact angle for the FCDs and CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids on several substrates (Fig. 3 a), given that catechol groups in FCDs can attach to various surfaces. We found that the contact angle were decreased for PP, PET, and poly(vinyl chloride) (PVC) surfaces but increased for Si wafers, indicating increased hydrophobicity. This suggested that the designed CsWO3–FCD could easily be used for detecting bacteria on a coated surface. We then evaluated the stability of the CsWO3–FCD-coated surface against water and bacterial media (LB solution). We observed that CsWO3–FCD-coated Si wafers demonstrated coating stability according to the insignificant changes in the contact angle for up to 10 days (Fig. S11a, b). The successful coating of the CsWO3–FCD was further observed using SEM-EDX (Fig. S12). The three-dimensional surface plot projected from the SEM image illustrated a difference in surface roughness between the bare Si wafer (Fig. S12a) and the CsWO3–FCD-coated Si wafer (Fig. S12b), with the CsWO3–FCD-coated Si wafer showing a rougher surface due to the surface distribution of CsWO3–FCDs. The EDX spectra further confirmed the presence of CsWO3–FCDs on the Si wafer surface according to the elemental spectra of C, O, Cs, and W (Fig. S12c). Additionally, atomic force microscopy images revealed different surface roughness and thickness before and after coating (Fig. S13a, b), with the post-coating thickness at 43.8 nm and confirming successful coating of CsWO3–FCD.

Luminescent-based bacterial detection was further verified using CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids coated on PP substrates and with the fluorescence-quenching phenomenon analyzed using confocal images. Fig. 3b shows the fluorescence images of a coated surface in the presence and absence of different bacterial concentrations. The blue-fluorescence emission of the coated surface was markedly reduced in the presence of both types of bacteria at ≥105 CFU/mL, whereas concentrations <105 CFU/mL continued to show substantial emission, and the quenching phenomenon still occurred, as compared with the coated surface in the absence of bacteria. Fluorescence quenching occurred due to the interaction between the negatively charged surface of the bacteria and cationic sites of the CsWO3-FCD-coated PP. These results demonstrated a bacterial detection method on a coated surface based on instantaneous changes in color.

We then examined the photothermal behavior of CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids coated on an Si surface. Fig. S14 shows that the Si surface coated with CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids displayed a large increase of temperature, which was related to the presence of NIR-responsive CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids on the surface. SEM analysis of both bacterial strains after treatment with the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids to determine the photothermal effect on bacteria after NIR irradiation (Figs. 4 and S15) clearly indicated the smooth surface of bacterial cells before NIR light exposure. Upon exposure of nanohybrid-treated bacteria to NIR irradiation, the bacterial surface was damaged by photothermolysis [50], leading to increases in observed dead bacteria according to SYTO9 and PI staining (Fig. S16a).

To confirm the role of the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids in NIR responsiveness and photothermolysis of bacteria, E. coli and S. aureus were seeded on unmodified Si wafers and irradiated with an NIR laser (2 W/cm2, 5 min), followed by live/dead staining and observation by confocal microscopy. Fig. S16b shows green fluorescence observed before and after NIR irradiation, verifying undamaged bacteria due to the absence of photothermolysis and confirming the role of the surface CsWO3–FCD in the photothermal killing of bacteria. Additionally, we examined the deformation of biofilm grown on the CsWO3–FCD-coated surface before and after NIR irradiation (5 min, 2 W/cm2) under confocal microscopy after live/dead staining. Fig. S16c shows deformation of the biofilms, with almost all bacterial cells killed (red fluorescence) and demonstrating the ability of the CsWO3–FCD coating to inhibit biofilm growth caused by bacteria.

To investigate the effect of photothermolysis on bacteria, we assessed bacterial cell-membrane integrity and leakage before and after NIR exposure using membrane-selective BODIPY-cholesterol dye (Fig. S17) [51,52]. As shown in Fig. S18a, the stained bacterial cell membrane (green fluorescence) showed good integrity in the absence of NIR exposure, whereas after 5 min of NIR irradiation, photothermolysis caused loss of cell-membrane integrity, resulting in leakage of intracellular compounds, including DNA. Confirmation of cell-membrane leakage was undertaken by evaluating the amount of leaked DNA according to the optical density at 260 nm (OD260) [53]. As shown in Fig. S18b, the OD260 value was higher after NIR irradiation relative to before NIR irradiation, confirming that photothermolysis occurred after NIR irradiation. These results verified that NIR exposure triggered photothermal heat generation by the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrids and caused photothermolysis of bacterial cells.

The LOD for the electrochemical measurement was obtained by plotting the linear curve between bacterial concentration and resistance value (Fig. S19), resulting in LODs of 3.81 CFU/mL for E. coli (R2 = 0.9972) and 5.15 CFU/mL for S. aureus (R2 = 0.9958). Compared with luminescent-based bacterial detection, the electrochemical approach was more sensitive. Luminescent-based detection of bacteria is inefficient relative to electrochemical-based detection based on the low sensitivity indicated by the higher LOD value. Additionally, unlike the electrochemical measurement, luminescent-based detection suffers from the intrinsic fluorescence properties of non-target molecules, which cause false-positive results and high background noise [55]. The LOD of the proposed biosensor showed excellent sensitivity relative to other methods (PCR, fluorescence, electrochemical, and electrochemiluminescence), with LOD value for electrochemical approach was lower than reported sensors within a linear range of bacterial concentration (101–107 CFU/mL) (Table S2) [56–63]. These results suggest the efficacy of the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrid as a biosensor for highly sensitive detection of gram-positive and -negative bacteria. Moreover, the electrochemical approach demonstrated good detection of bacteria at increasing concentrations (101–107 CFU/mL), even when the size of the sensor was changed, which would be advantageous for variable designs of the device according to the amount of bacteria needing to be captured (Fig. S20).

We then evaluated the selectivity of electrochemical-based detection in the presence of various types of interference, including Cl−, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, Br−, NH4 +, glutamic acid, and a mixture of these elements (matrix). Compared with the control (a CsWO3–FCD-coated Si wafer alone), the resistance of the CsWO3–FCD-coated Si wafer in the presence of each element remained unchanged, as well as in the complex matrix (Fig. S21a); however, the resistance increased dramatically upon inclusion of E. coli or S. aureus to the matrix. This result demonstrated the selectivity of this biosensor toward bacteria in a complex matrix comprising elements potentially capable of interfering with biosensor activity. We then evaluated biosensor performance using a real-world environmental sample (i.e., contaminated river water). As shown in Fig. S21b, the resistance of the CsWO3–FCD-coated Si wafer in contact with the river water increased significantly relative to that observed in the presence of sterilized DDW (control). This result confirmed that the biosensor was able to detect bacteria in a complex environmental sample, thereby demonstrating its efficacy for environmental applications.

Reusability is an important characteristic for practical application of biosensors. Therefore, we assessed the reusability of the CsWO3-FCD-coated Si wafer after several cycles. Following use for bacterial detection, the biosensor was recycled by applying pH shock using PBS (pH 3.0), followed by use for further electrochemical-based bacterial detection. Fig. S22 shows that the reused CsWO3–FCD-coated Si wafer displayed good performance at detecting E. coli and S. aureus (103 CFU/mL), even after five cycles, thereby confirming that the CsWO3–FCD-coated Si wafer possessed excellent reusability.

Based on the electrochemical response of the sensor toward different bacterial concentrations, we designed an electronic wireless sensor to track the presence of bacteria. Fig. 7 a illustrates the simple concept of bacterial detection using this wireless device. The CsWO3–FCD-coated Si wafer was connected to a Bluetooth module and a micro-controller. By activating the Bluetooth connection, the micro-controller then directly transmitted changes in biosensor resistance in the presence of bacteria to a smartphone, resulting in real-time bacteria detection. Fig. 7b demonstrates the complete setup for the wireless system which consists of a smartphone (receiver), a connector between the coated Si wafer and the circuit, and a circuit that includes a Bluetooth prism and a micro-controller. After connecting to the device, the presence of bacteria (displayed as a resistance graph) is monitored on the smartphone display (Fig. 7c). To test this system, we used the CsWO3–FCD-coated Si wafer as a control, and upon incubation with bacteria of various concentrations (101–107 CFU/mL), we observed concentration-dependent increases in resistance. This result agreed with LCR and EIS measurements (Fig. 5 and Fig. 6), which indicated that the electrochemical-based bacterial-sensing system could be combined with a wireless device to provide real-time results of bacterial detection directly to a smartphone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantage of CsWO3–FCDs as compared with CsWO3 and FCDs alone
ce:section.ce:para: Construction of the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrid combined the specific characteristics of FCDs and the CsWO3 nanoparticle to create a system capable of simultaneously detecting and eradicating bacteria. FCDs are widely used for bacterial sensing due to their luminescent property, electroconductivity, biocompatibility, solubility, and functionalization; however, most FCDs have no photothermal responsiveness capable of bacterial photothermolysis. CsWO3 nanoparticles exhibit excellent NIR-responsive photothermal effects capable of triggering bacterial photothermolysis; however, their use is hindered by lack of solubility and an ability to apply a coating. Combining FCDs and CsWO3 nanoparticles into a nanohybrid demonstrated simultaneous sensing and killing of bacteria along with good solubility and a coatable surface. Fig. S23 illustrates the solubility, coating ability, photothermal effect, and sensing ability of the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrid relative to these characteristics for FCDs and CsWO3 nanoparticles alone. Immobilization of the CsWO3 nanoparticle onto catechol-functionalized FCDs improved the solubility of the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrid relative to that of the CsWO3 nanoparticle (Fig. S23a). Furthermore, the presence of catechol provided an adhesive property for the surface coating. Fig. S23b shows that the FCDs and the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrid were successfully coated on the bare Si wafer according to the change in water-contact angle, whereas the CsWO3 nanoparticle could not be coated onto the Si wafer. Evaluation of the photothermal effect under NIR irradiation (5 min, 2 W/cm2) indicated that the NIR responsiveness of CsWO3 clearly affected photothermal generation on the nanoparticle and the CsWO3–FCD according to temperature elevation after a 5-min irradiation, which did not occur on the FCDs alone (Fig. S23c). Furthermore, the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrid demonstrated excellent bacterial sensing based on luminescent-based detection (λex max = 400 nm), which was not observed in the CsWO3 nanoparticle alone (Fig. S23d). These results confirmed the advantages of the CsWO3–FCD nanohybrid as compared with use of FCDs and CsWO3 nanoparticles alone for simultaneous bacterial detection and eradication.

Here, we described the design and evaluation of a surface-coatable, NIR-responsive, electrochemically generated CsWO3–FCD nanohybrid for wireless electrochemical- and luminescent-based bacterial detection in solutions and on coated surfaces. The biosensor utilizes catechol moieties to immobilize the CsWO3 nanoparticle onto FCDs and as an adhesive surface-coating agent. Bacterial detection was achieved using fluorescence quenching and the change in resistance that occurs due to the interaction between the cationic moiety of the FCDs and the anionic bacterial cell wall. The LOD for the fluorescence-based assay was 70 CFU/mL for E. coli and 131 CFU/mL for S. aureus, whereas the electrochemical approach was more sensitive, with LODs <10 CFU/mL for both bacterial strains. Furthermore, the electrochemical approach was expanded to allow real-time wireless bacterial sensing, with results displayed directly on a smartphone. Additionally, owing to the existence of NIR-responsive CsWO3 nanoparticles, the nanohybrid exhibited outstanding bacteria-killing ability under NIR exposure, with almost 100% killing efficiency toward E. coli and S. aureus. Thus, this CsWO3–FCD nanohybrid can be used to simultaneously detect and kill bacteria both in solutions and on coated surfaces using either luminescent or electrochemical approaches.
